mockBank.question: A patient with a known penicillin allergy requires an antibiotic for a dental abscess. They also have a history of C. difficile colitis from a previous antibiotic course. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate antibiotic?
options: A. Clindamycin 150mg four times daily
B. Metronidazole 400mg three times daily
C. Clarithromycin 250mg twice daily
D. Co-amoxiclav 625mg three times daily
E. Cephalexin 500mg four times daily
mockBank.explanation: Metronidazole is the best choice here: it is suitable for penicillin allergy, targets the likely anaerobic organisms in a dental abscess, and — critically — is used to TREAT C. difficile infection, not cause it. Clindamycin (A) is notorious for causing C. difficile pseudomembranous colitis and is absolutely avoided in this patient.

mockBank.question: A patient with severe alcoholic hepatitis is seen for an emergency dental extraction. Before administering sedation, you review their medications and note they are on diazepam for anxiety. Which ONE of the following concerns is MOST important?
options: A. Diazepam may reduce local anaesthetic efficacy
B. Diazepam is not metabolised in liver disease and will accumulate, potentially causing toxicity and coma
C. Diazepam increases bleeding risk through platelet inhibition
D. Diazepam is contraindicated with adrenaline in local anaesthetics
E. The combination of diazepam and alcohol is only a concern if the patient actively drinks during the appointment
mockBank.explanation: Benzodiazepines are extensively metabolised in the liver. In severe liver disease, hepatic clearance is dramatically impaired, causing drug accumulation. Even prescribed therapeutic doses of benzodiazepines can precipitate hepatic encephalopathy or coma in cirrhotic patients. Conscious sedation should be avoided unless in a specialist unit.

mockBank.question: Interferon alpha is used as an antiviral agent. Which ONE of the following BEST describes its mechanism of action?
options: A. It directly inhibits viral RNA polymerase
B. It binds to specific viral surface glycoproteins to prevent cell entry
C. It is a cytokine that enhances cytotoxic T-lymphocyte activity and induces antiviral protein synthesis in surrounding cells
D. It acts as a competitive inhibitor of viral protease
E. It prevents viral DNA integration into the host genome
mockBank.explanation: Interferons are glycoproteins produced by the body in response to viral infection. They act on neighbouring cells to induce an antiviral state — upregulating MHC class I presentation, activating NK cells, and enhancing cytotoxic T-lymphocyte activity. They were historically used for hepatitis B and C treatment.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following molecular diagnostic techniques is MOST appropriate for diagnosing a CHRONIC infection such as HIV?
options: A. PCR detecting viral RNA
B. Serology detecting antibodies to the virus
C. Viral culture on specific cell lines
D. Electron microscopy of peripheral blood
E. Direct antigen detection by ELISA on a swab sample
mockBank.explanation: Serology (antibody detection) is preferred for chronic infections — the body has had time to mount an antibody response that becomes progressively stronger. PCR is better for acute infections (early after exposure, before seroconversion) or when quantifying viral load. For HIV, a combination of p24 antigen and antibody testing (4th generation test) is now used.

mockBank.question: A patient being treated for pericoronitis develops double vision and severe headaches while taking metronidazole. They stop the drug and symptoms resolve. Which ONE of the following is the MOST appropriate action for the prescribing dentist?
options: A. Re-prescribe metronidazole at a lower dose
B. Prescribe an alternative antibiotic and report the adverse reaction via the Yellow Card scheme
C. Refer the patient to neurology for investigation of their visual symptoms
D. Notify the patient's GP by telephone only
E. Document the reaction in the patient's notes and take no further action
mockBank.explanation: The prescribing dentist should: (1) prescribe an alternative antibiotic to treat the ongoing infection; (2) submit a Yellow Card report to the MHRA — double vision and severe headaches are unexpected serious adverse drug reactions that must be formally reported. Documentation in patient notes is also required but insufficient on its own.

mockBank.question: When requesting a laboratory investigation for a dental infection, which ONE of the following samples provides the MOST useful information for both organism identification and antibiotic sensitivity?
options: A. Surface swab from the gingival margin
B. Charcoal swab from the oral mucosa
C. Aspirate of pus from the abscess
D. Viral transport medium swab
E. Blood culture only
mockBank.explanation: Aspirated pus or tissue provides a much better sample than a swab — swabs pick up surface organisms and may miss the causative pathogen. Pus aspirate allows both culture and antibiotic susceptibility testing on the actual infecting organisms. Anaerobes may also die in swabs.

mockBank.question: After completing nitrous oxide sedation, the patient should receive 100% oxygen for a minimum of 3 minutes. Which ONE of the following BEST explains the reason for this?
options: A. To neutralise residual nitrous oxide molecules in the blood
B. To prevent diffusion hypoxia — large volumes of N₂O leaving the blood rapidly can displace oxygen from the alveoli
C. To reverse the amnesic effects of nitrous oxide
D. To compensate for the tachycardia caused by nitrous oxide
E. Because 100% oxygen is required by CQC regulations following all inhalation sedation
mockBank.explanation: When N₂O administration stops, its poor blood solubility causes rapid elimination via the lungs. The large volume of N₂O pouring from the blood into the alveoli can displace oxygen, causing diffusion hypoxia (the patient effectively breathes very high-concentration N₂O briefly). Three minutes of 100% O₂ prevents this.

mockBank.question: Which ONE of the following is NOT an appropriate indication for prophylactic antibiotic prescribing in dentistry?
options: A. Prevention of wound infection in a patient with previous head and neck radiotherapy undergoing tooth extraction
B. Prophylaxis against infective endocarditis in a patient with a prosthetic heart valve — routinely before extractions
C. Prevention of infection following bone grafting for a dental implant in a compromised patient
D. Prevention of oro-antral communication infection when extracting an upper molar
E. Prevention of wound infection in a bone marrow transplant recipient requiring oral surgery
mockBank.explanation: NICE (2016) guidelines explicitly state that antibiotic prophylaxis against infective endocarditis is NOT routinely recommended before dental procedures, even in high-risk patients with prosthetic valves. The risk of anaphylaxis from antibiotic prophylaxis may exceed the risk of IE from a single dental procedure.
